subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Exact component counts from the provided rulebook text: 500 cards total. Treasure cards: 130 (60 Copper, 40 Silver, 30 Gold). Victory cards: 48 (24 Estate, 12 Duchy, 12 Province). Curse cards: 30. Kingdom cards: 252. Randomizer cards: 32. Trash card: 1. Blank cards: 7. The rulebook also specifies 25 different Kingdom card types, with setup normally using 10 selected Kingdom piles each game. Most Kingdom piles have 10 cards each. Exception from the rules text: Kingdom Victory piles (example: Gardens) use the same pile size as base Victory piles (12 in 3-4 player, 8 in 2 player). Every game uses the base Treasure, base Victory, Curse, and Trash area in addition to the chosen Kingdom piles.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player begins with exactly 10 cards: 7 Coppers and 3 Estates. Each player shuffles those 10 cards to form their starting deck and places it face down in their play area. Then each player draws 5 cards as their opening hand. No player starts with any Action cards in deck or hand by default setup, so early turns are generally treasure-driven. Each player maintains three personal zones through play: deck (face down), discard pile (face up), and play area for cards played this turn.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: 1) Build the Supply with all base Treasure piles (Copper, Silver, Gold), base Victory piles (Estate, Duchy, Province), Curse pile, Trash area, and 10 selected Kingdom piles. 2) Base Victory pile sizes in Supply are player-count dependent: in 2-player games, 8 each of Estate/Duchy/Province; in 3-4 player games, 12 each. 3) Curse count in Supply is 10 (2-player), 20 (3-player), or 30 (4-player). 4) Choose a starting player randomly (or in repeated games, player to left of prior winner as described). 5) Play proceeds clockwise from the starting player.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
A turn is always resolved in this numbered order:
1. Action Phase
   - You may play one Action card from hand.
   - If Action cards grant +Actions, you may play additional Action cards up to remaining Action count.
   - You must fully resolve each Action card before playing the next one.
2. Buy Phase
   - Play any number of Treasure cards from hand to your play area.
   - Add coin values from Treasures plus any +coins generated by Action cards this turn.
   - Buy one card from Supply by default (or more if +Buys were generated).
   - Purchased cards go to discard pile, not hand, unless an effect says otherwise.
3. Clean-up Phase
   - Discard all cards in play area (Actions/Treasures played this turn).
   - Discard all cards remaining in your hand.
   - Draw 5 new cards.
   - If deck is short while drawing, draw what you can, shuffle discard to form new deck, then continue drawing.
After clean-up, turn ends and passes clockwise.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Action play rules:
- Playing an Action is optional even if you have one in hand.
- Standard limit is one Action in Action Phase, modified only by +Actions.
- You must do as much of the played card as possible, even if some instructions cannot be fully completed.
- Action cards stay in play area until Clean-up unless card text says otherwise.
- Reaction cards are a defined exception and can be used outside your Action phase when triggered by card text.

Common card terms from the rulebook and their exact functional meaning:
- +X Cards: immediately draw X cards (shuffling discard into deck if needed).
- +X Actions: increases total Actions available during Action phase; does not interrupt current resolution.
- +X coins: adds spendable coins for this turn's Buy phase without taking extra Treasure cards.
- +1 Buy: increases number of purchases you may make in Buy phase.
- Discard: place specified cards from hand (unless text says otherwise) face up onto discard pile.
- Trash: place card(s) in Trash pile, not discard; trashed cards are not in Supply and cannot be bought.
- Gain: take card (usually from Supply) and place into discard unless effect specifies different destination.
- Reveal: show card(s) to all players, then return to source location unless told otherwise.
- Set Aside: place card(s) aside temporarily until card text tells where they go.

Buy options and restrictions:
- You may buy any card in Supply that you can afford, including Treasure, Victory, Kingdom, and Curse cards.
- You cannot buy cards from Trash pile.
- You may combine coin value from all Treasures played plus +coins generated by Actions.
- If you have multiple Buys, you may split coins across purchases however you choose, as long as each buy is legal and affordable at purchase moment.
- You may choose not to use all Buys or all coins.

Card flow reminders that matter for rules disputes:
- Gained/bought cards are not used immediately unless text explicitly overrides this.
- Treasures are income engines: they are discarded in Clean-up but will cycle back when discard is reshuffled.
- Top card of each discard pile is always visible public information.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
1) Shared Supply, private deck engine
Dominion's core mechanism is that all players buy from shared market piles (Supply), but each player's deck composition evolves independently. Your deck is both your economy and your action menu, so every buy has delayed impact.

2) Buy vs Gain distinction
The rules explicitly separate buying from gaining. Buying happens in Buy phase and spends a Buy. Gaining can happen from card effects, usually to discard, without spending a Buy unless text says otherwise. Many beginner timing errors come from mixing these two terms.

3) Strict phase gates with card-based expansion
You cannot normally buy during Action phase or play new Actions during Buy phase. However, Action cards can expand what you can do later in turn (+Actions, +Buys, +coins, +Cards). Sequence discipline is critical: resolve current Action completely before starting another.

4) Deck cycling and shuffle timing
When instructed to draw and your deck is insufficient, draw remaining cards from deck, then shuffle discard to form new deck, then finish the draw. This timing matters for cards that draw multiple times in one turn and for planning collision of newly bought cards into future hands.

5) Visible discard top and hidden hand information
Rule text emphasizes discard top-card visibility while hand contents remain hidden unless revealed. This affects attack/reaction reads and turn planning in interactive kingdoms.

6) Kingdom set variability
Each game uses exactly 10 selected Kingdom piles out of 25 card types in the listed edition, producing different strategic landscapes. Rulebook recommends a starter set for first game (Cellar, Market, Militia, Mine, Moat, Remodel, Smithy, Village, Woodcutter, Workshop). As players gain experience, randomizer selection and curated sets create different tempo and interaction profiles.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
End trigger and scoring structure for base Dominion in the provided rules context:
- Players keep taking full turns in order until an end condition is met by Supply depletion (standard base-game structure uses Province depletion and/or pile depletion thresholds depending player count and setup conventions).
- When game ends, each player totals victory points from all cards they own in deck, hand, discard, and cards in play.

Victory point sources from the provided component/rule text:
- Estate: 1 VP each.
- Duchy: 3 VP each.
- Province: 6 VP each.
- Curse: -1 VP each.
- Any Kingdom Victory cards included in the chosen 10-pile setup also score according to their own printed text.

Winner determination:
- Highest total VP wins.
- If tied, tie is resolved by official turn-order/tie procedures from Dominion rules set in use (commonly fewer turns among tied players). If still tied after applying the official tie rule, tied players share victory.

Practical scoring checklist:
1) Combine all owned zones into one count (deck + hand + discard + in-play).
2) Count all positive VP cards.
3) Subtract Curse penalties.
4) Apply any Kingdom-specific VP conditions from selected sets.
5) Compare totals and apply tie procedure.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) First buys should improve deck function, not just raw points. In most kingdoms, early VP purchases (especially Estates) weaken hand quality and reduce your ability to buy stronger cards on turns 3-6.
2) Identify your draw and action backbone immediately. Look for reliable +Cards and +Actions so future turns can play multiple impactful cards rather than stalling with dead terminal Actions.
3) Build a money floor early. Even in action-heavy setups, consistent coin generation (via Treasures and/or coin-producing Actions) prevents hands that do nothing.
4) Prioritize cards that improve future turns, not only current turn. Cards that draw, gain, or filter often outperform one-shot effects in the opening.
5) Track what your first shuffle will contain. Cards bought on turns 1 and 2 define your first real deck cycle; choose them as a pair, not as isolated purchases.
6) If interaction cards are present (attacks, reactions), decide early whether to race, defend, or out-scale; reactive pivots too late can lose multiple turns of tempo.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Buying green too early
Why it's a mistake: Victory cards add points but usually do not help your turn when drawn, reducing economy and action density before your engine is ready.

2) Overloading on terminal Actions
Why it's a mistake: Without enough +Actions, you can only play one Action in Action phase, so extra terminal cards clog hand and waste buys.

3) Ignoring draw-to-buy timing
Why it's a mistake: New players often treat every +coin as equivalent, but hands fail when card draw and Action availability are not aligned with payload turns.

4) Forgetting that gained/bought cards usually go to discard
Why it's a mistake: Planning as if a bought card can be used this turn causes sequencing errors and bad purchases.

5) Not watching pile pressure
Why it's a mistake: Dominion can end suddenly when key piles empty; if you keep building engine while end triggers approach, you may lose with a "better" deck but fewer points.

6) Underusing Trash effects
Why it's a mistake: Trashing weak starter cards is one of the fastest ways to improve consistency. Avoiding trash tools often leaves your deck diluted.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
A) Engine growth vs scoring pivot
- Continue buying engine cards for stronger future turns, or start buying VP now before end triggers fire.
- Heuristic: pivot when your average turn can reliably buy meaningful VP and there are not many cycles left.

B) Reliability vs explosiveness
- Buy stable money/draw cards that work every hand, or buy high-ceiling combo cards needing specific support.
- Heuristic: in interactive or attack-heavy kingdoms, reliability often beats fragile combo plans.

C) Endgame control vs raw point gain
- Decide whether to force game end (pile pressure/province race) or prolong game to exploit your superior engine.
- Heuristic: if ahead on conversion rate, extend; if behind on scaling but close in points, accelerate end triggers.

metadata.notes: Built from provided Dominion rulebook excerpt with explicit component counts, phase structure, term definitions, and setup rules.
